fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 74. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 70.

course_description.character: A point-to-point Andean road ascent beginning at Estanques, approximately 426 m above sea level, passing through Mérida's Sierra Nevada corridor (shorter-distance fields join from Mérida, Valle Hermoso and Mucuchíes) and finishing at Collado del Cóndor/Pico El Águila at 4,118 m — not the 4,200 m previously on file, which appears to have rounded the finish altitude rather than measuring it precisely. The defining feature is the roughly 3,692 m lowland-to-páramo altitude transition from start to finish, with some descents interspersed before the final high-altitude climb rather than one uninterrupted ascent. Total climbing gain (as opposed to net elevation change) is not independently confirmed.
course_description.signature_challenge: Mucuchíes-Collado — the final 26 km, climbing from 2,983 m to 4,118 m (about 1,135 m net), increasingly exposed to hypoxia and low páramo temperatures as the finish approaches.
suffering_zones: Estanques-Mérida — roughly the opening 43 km, climbing from the 426 m lowland start toward Mérida at approximately 1,530 m
Valle Hermoso-Mucuchíes — approximately 20 km between the 46 km and 26 km starting points, rising from 2,206 m to 2,983 m (about 777 m net)
The full lowland-to-páramo transition — a genuinely exceptional 3,692 m start-to-finish altitude swing rather than sustained continuous climbing throughout

history.origin_story: Part of the Gran Fondo World Tour series; the 2026 edition was its sixth running.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Genuine Altitude Monument — With a Real Travel-Risk Question Attached
biased_opinion.summary: Gran Fondo Collado del Condor delivers a real and exceptional altitude test: a roughly 3,692 m net lowland-to-páramo climb from Estanques (426 m) to Collado del Cóndor/Pico El Águila (4,118 m). Guinness World Records recognizes it (with Ultrabikex) for the 'Longest hill climb bicycle race,' set at 123.5 km on 23 March 2025. The 2026 sixth edition drew 1,000+ riders across four distance options with full event-day road closure and security. What sets this event apart from most others in this database isn't the course — it's the destination. As of this research, UK Foreign Office travel advice recommends against all but essential travel to Mérida and most of Venezuela, with limited flights and constrained emergency support. That's a genuine, practical planning factor for international riders considering this event, separate from the course itself.
strengths: Exceptional 3,692 m start-to-finish altitude swing — a genuinely rare format among gran fondos
Four accessible distance choices (123/80/46/26 km) let riders opt into less of the altitude exposure
1,000+ rider scale with full event-day road closure and security deployment
Guinness World Record holder for the 'Longest hill climb bicycle race' (with Ultrabikex), set 23 March 2025
weaknesses: Published distance is inconsistent — 123 km in 2026 results versus 125 km on the Gran Fondo World Tour's own page — and no reliable source confirms total climbing gain (as distinct from net elevation change)
The altitude demands real acclimatization; 2026 also followed páramo road damage from severe rain

As of this research, UK government travel advice recommends against all but essential travel to Mérida and most of Venezuela, with limited flights and constrained emergency support — a genuine logistical and safety factor for international riders to weigh independently, not a reason to avoid the event outright
The organizer's Gran Fondo World Tour page carries placeholder text and limited operational detail beyond the core route
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A genuinely exceptional altitude test that delivers exactly what it promises on the bike — but international riders should independently check current travel advisories for Venezuela before committing to travel, separate from evaluating the course itself.

final_verdict.one_liner: Guinness World Record holder for longest hill climb bicycle race — a genuine altitude monument, though international riders should check current Venezuela travel advisories before booking.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes for the altitude test itself, which is genuinely exceptional. Check current government travel advisories for Venezuela independently before planning international travel — this is a real logistical factor separate from the race.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similarly extreme high-altitude gran fondo with more straightforward international travel logistics, compare against other Andean or high-Alpine altitude events in this database.

eligibility.notes: Sixth edition ran 22 March 2026 with 1,000+ finishers/participants reported; a March 2027 return is planned. Vitals corrected: distance 123 km (down from 125.2 km), elevation reframed as 3,692 m net start-to-finish gain (426 m start to 4,118 m finish) rather than a flat 4,200 m figure, which conflated finish altitude with climbing total. Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research as part of the Roadie Labs editorial wave 3.
